mission.title: Install and Configure MySQL Operator for Kubernetes (Oracle)
mission.description: The MySQL Operator for Kubernetes automates the management of MySQL InnoDB Cluster setups within a Kubernetes environment. It simplifies tasks such as deployment, scaling, and backup, making it ideal for users who require a robust database solution integrated with Kubernetes. Key trade-offs include the need for proper configuration and potential complexity in managing custom resources.

steps.title: Add Helm Repository
steps.description:
This step adds the MySQL Operator Helm repository to your local Helm configuration, allowing you to install the operator using Helm.

```bash

helm repo add mysql-operator https://mysql.github.io/mysql-operator/

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the official MySQL Operator Helm repository.
> This command updates your local Helm repository cache.

steps.title: Install MySQL Operator
steps.description:
This step installs the MySQL Operator in the Kubernetes cluster using Helm, creating a dedicated namespace for it.

```bash

helm install mysql-operator mysql-operator/mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator --create-namespace

```

> This command deploys the MySQL Operator with default configurations.

steps.title: Verify Operator Deployment
steps.description:
This step checks if the MySQL Operator is running correctly by querying the deployment status in the specified namespace.

```bash

kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator

```

> You should see the operator deployment with 1/1 replicas ready.

steps.title: Create MySQL Root User Secret
steps.description:
This step creates a Kubernetes secret that contains the credentials for the MySQL root user, which is necessary for managing the MySQL InnoDB Cluster.

```bash

kubectl create secret generic mypwds --from-literal=rootUser=root --from-literal=rootHost=% --from-literal=rootPassword: <REDACTED>'

```

> Replace 'sakila' with a secure password.

steps.title: Define MySQL InnoDB Cluster
steps.description:
This step defines a MySQL InnoDB Cluster custom resource, which specifies the configuration for your MySQL cluster.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.4'
EOF

```

> This command creates an InnoDB Cluster named 'mycluster'.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.4'
EOF

steps.title: Verify InnoDB Cluster Creation
steps.description:
This step checks the status of the InnoDB Cluster to ensure it has been created successfully.

```bash

kubectl get innodbclusters.mysql.oracle.com mycluster

```

> You should see the status of your InnoDB Cluster.

uninstall.title: Uninstall MySQL Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the MySQL Operator from your Kubernetes cluster. Be aware that this will also delete any associated resources, including the InnoDB Cluster and its data.

```bash

helm uninstall mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator

kubectl delete namespace mysql-operator

```

> This command removes the MySQL Operator deployment.
> This command deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade MySQL Operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you backup your MySQL data. This step will upgrade the MySQL Operator to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl get innodbclusters.mysql.oracle.com -o json > backup-innodbclusters.json

helm upgrade mysql-operator mysql-operator/mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator

kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator

```

> Backup existing InnoDB Cluster configurations.
> This command upgrades the MySQL Operator to the latest version.
> Verify the operator is running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create InnoDB Cluster'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the secret for MySQL root user is not created or is misconfigured.

**Fix:**
Ensure the secret is created correctly with the right credentials.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'MySQL Operator not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the MySQL Operator deployment is not running.

**Fix:**
Check the deployment status with 'kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient resources to create MySQL pod'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may happen if the cluster does not have enough resources allocated.

**Fix:**
Ensure your Kubernetes nodes meet the minimum resource requirements (2 vCPU, 4GB RAM).

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to MySQL'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can occur if the MySQL service is not properly exposed or the cluster is misconfigured.

**Fix:**
Check the service configuration and ensure it is accessible.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for MySQL 8.4 and improved backup functionalities.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 8.4, ensure to update your InnoDB Cluster configurations to match the new API version.

resolution.summary: You should see the InnoDB Cluster status as 'Ready' when everything is working: 'kubectl get innodbclusters.mysql.oracle.com mycluster'.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.4'
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure your Kubernetes cluster meets the resource and tool requirements before installation.
